dataset: Dyna-1 paper's own curated set (Wayment-Steele, El Nesr et al. 2025)
note: Apples-to-apples. Use their held-out split so you're not scoring on training residues.

observable: R1, R2, hetNOE, CPMG relaxation dispersion, R1ρ, CEST, S²
note: The primary NMR repository. µs–ms dispersion coverage is sparse and heterogeneous — you harvest it, it isn't pre-tabulated.

dataset: Canonical Kern-lab systems: Cyclophilin A, Adenylate kinase, PTP1B, DHFR, RNase A, Abl/Src
observable: CPMG + long MD + solved ensembles, all published
note: These are exactly your benchmark's Tier-A systems. Best-characterized µs–ms exchange in the literature.

observable: All-atom MD (3×100 ns), per-residue RMSF / flexibility
note: ~1500 proteins, standardized. RMSF is dominated by fast motion; use as a flexibility baseline, not a µs–ms proxy.
Large coverage. Good for RMSF baselines and for building slow-mode (tICA/MSM) metrics yourself.

dataset: DE Shaw / Anton ultra-long MD (BPTI 1 ms; fast folders, Lindorff-Larsen 2011)
note: Only source that actually reaches ms in MD — but you must extract µs–ms modes via MSM/tICA, not raw RMSF.
Great for disordered/flexible regions; weaker for compact folded µs–ms exchange.

dataset: CoDNaS (conformational diversity of the native state)
observable: RMSD between alternative solved states of the same protein
note: Tells you which residues move between crystallized states; no timescale.

dataset: qFit multiconformer models + crystallographic B-factors / ensemble refinement
note: B-factors confounded by lattice, resolution, refinement. Alt-confs are a better hint than raw B.
Protection factor convolves dynamics with local stability; different regime than µs–ms exchange.
Cryptic sites tend to sit in mobile regions; useful cross-check, not the same variable.
Use as a NEGATIVE control: a good Dyna-1 must predict µs–ms exchange that S² does NOT already explain.
